name: Acma
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Display, Expressive, Humanist, Sans Serif, Variable. A high-contrast narrow sans serif aimed at editorial headlines, with an alternate set of wide caps and lowercase swaps for unexpected moments.
summary: A high-contrast narrow display sans by Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins, drawing on Japanese modernism and fashion editorial.
description: A narrow, high-contrast sans by Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins. Twenty styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics and a variable cut. The design carries deep contrast, luscious curves, and a wide alternate cap set, intended for editorial headlines but usable from 12pt upward. Pangram positions it for fashion, beauty, and identity work that wants opulence without losing precision.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Acma as an irreverent typeface with a powerful elegance, inspired by Japanese modernism and the fashion world for its unapologetic nature and opulence. They cite the design of the lowercase a as the family's anchor, with luscious curves, wide alternates, and deep contrast across ten weights plus a variable cut.

name: Agrandir
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The full Agrandir collection spans six subfamilies across five widths plus a Text cut, with stylistic alternates and Default, Grotesk, and Geometric presets.
summary: A 74-font expressive sans system by Alex Slobzheninov, spanning five widths plus a Text cut for body copy.
description: An expressive sans system by Alex Slobzheninov, built across seven weights and five widths with italics and a four-style Text cut. Geometric, Grotesk, and Default presets switch the alternates wholesale, swapping single-storey a, alternate y, u, G, K and a straight-tail R. Pangram pitches it for headlines, websites, logos and posters, with the Text styles holding up at body sizes.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Agrandir as a contemporary serifless type family that celebrates the beauty of being imperfect, designed as a brave antipode to neutral modernist fonts. They count 74 fonts across seven weights, five widths, italics and four Text styles, plus a three-axis variable, with stylistic presets they label Default, Grotesk and Geometric.

name: Agrandir Grand
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The widest cut in the Agrandir system, sharing the family's alternate sets and Geometric / Grotesk presets.
summary: The widest width in Alex Slobzheninov's Agrandir system, built for big, expressive headline settings.
description: The Grand width of Agrandir, drawn by Alex Slobzheninov as the most extended cut in the system. Fourteen styles span seven weights from Thin to Black with italics and 443 glyphs per weight. The same alternates and stylistic presets carry through, so Grand can sit beside the Normal, Wide and Tight widths in a single family hierarchy.
foundry_blurb: Pangram place Agrandir Grand inside the wider Agrandir system, framed as the family that flips modernist neutrality on its head. They credit the Agrandir collection to designer Alex Slobzheninov and describe it as built for humans, not machines, with 14 weights and 443 glyphs each at this width.

name: Agrandir Narrow
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The narrow cut in the Agrandir system, sharing the family's alternates and Geometric / Grotesk presets.
summary: The narrow cut of Alex Slobzheninov's Agrandir, useful where you want personality at a tighter horizontal measure.
description: The Narrow width of Agrandir by Alex Slobzheninov, drawn as a tighter sibling of Normal and Tight. Fourteen styles run from Thin to Black with italics and 443 glyphs each. The alternate sets, single-storey a and Geometric/Grotesk presets carry through, so Narrow can flex inside a wider Agrandir system.
foundry_blurb: Pangram set Agrandir Narrow alongside the rest of the Agrandir collection, describing the family as a contemporary serifless type system that flips modernist neutrality on its head. They credit Alex Slobzheninov as the designer and note 14 weights and 443 glyphs each at this width.

name: Agrandir Normal
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The default width in the Agrandir system, holding the family's alternates and stylistic presets.
summary: The default width of Alex Slobzheninov's Agrandir, the entry point into the wider system.
description: The Normal width of Agrandir by Alex Slobzheninov, the family's default proportions. Fourteen styles span seven weights with italics and 443 glyphs each. Stylistic presets, single-storey a, alternate y, u, G, K and the straight-tail R run across the family, letting Normal sit cleanly with Tight, Narrow, Wide and Grand.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Agrandir as a contemporary serifless type family that celebrates the beauty of being imperfect. They credit Alex Slobzheninov as the designer of the system and note that this width carries 14 weights and 443 glyphs each, with italic support and the family's alternate sets.

name: Agrandir Text
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. A four-style text cut tuned for body copy, drawn as a companion to the wider Agrandir system.
summary: The four-style text cut of Alex Slobzheninov's Agrandir, tuned for running body copy.
description: The Text cut of Agrandir by Alex Slobzheninov, drawn for body sizes rather than display. Four styles cover Regular and Bold with matching italics, sharing the family's alternates and presets so a website or magazine can run Agrandir display headlines into Agrandir Text paragraphs without breaking voice.
foundry_blurb: Pangram pitch Agrandir Text as the body-copy companion inside the Agrandir collection, describing the wider family as built for humans, not machines. They credit Alex Slobzheninov as the designer and frame this cut as an intentional departure tuned for running text.

name: Agrandir Tight
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The tightest-spaced cut in the Agrandir system, drawn for big, dense headlines.
summary: The tightly-spaced cut of Alex Slobzheninov's Agrandir, tuned for dense display settings.
description: The Tight width of Agrandir by Alex Slobzheninov, the most compressed cut in the system. Fourteen styles span seven weights from Thin to Black with italics and 443 glyphs each. The family's alternates and presets carry through, so Tight handles posters and big display lockups while still talking to Normal and Wide in body settings.
foundry_blurb: Pangram place Agrandir Tight inside the Agrandir collection, describing the family as a contemporary serifless type system that flips modernist neutrality on its head. They credit Alex Slobzheninov as the designer and note 14 weights with italic support at this tightest width.

name: Agrandir Wide
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The wide cut in the Agrandir system, sitting between Normal and Grand.
summary: The wide cut of Alex Slobzheninov's Agrandir, sitting between Normal and Grand in the system.
description: The Wide width of Agrandir by Alex Slobzheninov, drawn as a roomier sibling of Normal. Fourteen styles run from Thin to Black with italics and 443 glyphs each, carrying the family's alternates and Geometric/Grotesk presets. Useful where you want extra horizontal presence without going all the way to Grand.
foundry_blurb: Pangram set Agrandir Wide alongside the rest of the Agrandir collection, describing the family as built for humans, not machines. They credit Alex Slobzheninov as the designer and note 14 weights and 443 glyphs each, with italic support, at this wider width.

name: Air
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Grotesk, Italics, Monospace, Sans Serif, Variable. The combined Air collection covers the proportional Sans family and a matching Mono cut.
summary: A brutalist-minimalist grotesk system by Mat Desjardins and Caio Kondo, with paired proportional and monospaced cuts.
description: A grotesk system by Mat Desjardins and Caio Kondo of Inari Type, paired across a proportional Sans and a matching Mono. Thirty-six weights cover Thin to Black with italics, plus a variable cut. The character map includes alternate letters, dense punctuation and a set of arrows, intended to push between minimal and experimental settings.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Air as a harmonious collision of brutalism, minimalism and aerospace, originally designed by Mat Desjardins and expanded by Caio Kondo from Inari Type. They highlight 36 weights in total, alternate characters, abundant punctuation and a set of arrows, plus a variable cut that unites the axes.

name: Air Mono
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Grotesk, Italics, Monospace, Sans Serif, Variable. The fixed-width sibling to Air Sans, with Mono, Workhorse and Relaxed stylistic sets.
summary: The fixed-width cut of Air by Mat Desjardins and Caio Kondo, with Mono, Workhorse and Relaxed stylistic sets.
description: The monospaced cut of Air by Mat Desjardins and Caio Kondo of Inari Type. Eighteen styles run from Thin to Black with italics, with stylistic sets labelled Mono, Workhorse and Relaxed for different code and editorial uses. Discretionary ligatures cover fi, si, sr, st, Th, plus alternate symbols, standard ligatures and case-sensitive forms.
foundry_blurb: Pangram place Air Mono inside the Air collection, describing the family as a harmonious collision of brutalism, minimalism and aerospace originally designed by Mat Desjardins and expanded by Caio Kondo from Inari Type. They list Mono, Workhorse and Relaxed stylistic sets, with discretionary ligatures and case-sensitive forms.

name: Air Sans
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The proportional sibling to Air Mono, sharing the same stylistic sets and alternates.
summary: The proportional sans cut of Air by Mat Desjardins and Caio Kondo, paired with Air Mono.
description: The proportional sans cut of Air by Mat Desjardins and Caio Kondo of Inari Type. Eighteen styles run from Thin to Black with italics and a variable axis. Mono, Workhorse and Relaxed stylistic sets carry across to Air Mono, so a project can pair the two cuts cleanly through code, captions and headlines.
foundry_blurb: Pangram pitch Air Sans alongside Air Mono inside the Air collection, describing the wider family as a harmonious collision of brutalism, minimalism and aerospace. They credit Mat Desjardins as the original designer with Caio Kondo from Inari Type expanding the system, with stylistic sets and alternate characters running across both widths.

name: Casa Stencil
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Display, Expressive, Serif. A single-style stencil with soft curves and an eroded feel, drawn for titles and logos.
summary: A soft, eroded stencil display serif by Mat Desjardins, made for titles and logos.
description: A single-style stencil display serif by Mat Desjardins. The shapes carry soft curves and a sense of erosion, leaning elegant rather than industrial. Pangram pitch it for titles and logos, and the licence package includes eight digital flower posters as supporting graphics.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Casa Stencil as a beautiful, elegant stencil font with soft curves and a sense of erosion, made for titles and logos. They credit it to Mat Desjardins and bundle eight digital flower posters with all commercial licence purchases.

name: Charlevoix Pro
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Grotesk, Sans Serif. A nine-weight bold display sans inspired by the Quebec region of Charlevoix, with slightly rounded corners.
summary: A nine-weight rounded display sans by Mat Desjardins, drawn as a tribute to Quebec's Charlevoix region.
description: A bold display sans by Mat Desjardins, named after the Charlevoix region of Quebec. Nine weights span Thin to Black, all upright, with slightly rounded corners that take the edge off the heavier cuts. Pangram pitch it for titles where you want strength and softness in the same lockup.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Charlevoix Pro as a bold display typeface that blends strength with softness, taking visual cues from the dramatic landscapes of Quebec's Charlevoix region. They credit it to Mat Desjardins and frame it as nature-shaped and display-ready, with rounded corners giving it a human touch in titles.

name: Chronos Serif
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Display, Expressive, Serif. A three-variant display serif: Stroked, Hatched, and Filled, each with its own visual treatment.
summary: A three-variant display serif by Mat Desjardins, with Stroked, Hatched and Filled treatments.
description: A display serif by Mat Desjardins, drawn in three sibling cuts: Stroked, Hatched and Filled. Each variant carries its own surface treatment over the same skeleton, so a luxury or editorial system can swap textures without losing the underlying letterform. Pangram pitch it as a high-end title and packaging face.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Chronos Serif as an elegant display serif with three variants, Stroked, Hatched and Filled, each with its own flavour. They credit it to Mat Desjardins and frame it as time-honoured elegance loaded with details and elaborate curves, intended to uplift high-end projects.

name: Cirka
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Brutalist, Expressive, Serif and Variable. A vertical-axis serif with sharp, pointy wedge serifs, drawn for posters and headlines but also usable at text size.
summary: Sharp, wedge-serifed display serif with a vertical axis, drawn by Nick Losacco for posters and headlines.
description: A vertical-axis serif by Nick Losacco with pointy, spiky wedge serifs and a flamboyant character. Five weights from Light to Bold cover most editorial and identity uses, working large on posters as well as smaller in body. Pangram cite the Lamborghini 'Italian Wedge' shape as a reference, which gives Cirka its hard, geometric edge.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Cirka as elegant and classical with sharp pointy serifs, sitting on a vertical axis. They credit Nick Losacco as designer and reference the Lamborghini 'Italian Wedge' shape as a visual cue. The family is offered in five styles plus a variable font.

name: Editorial New
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Italics, Serif and Variable. A precise narrow serif drawn for long-form copy with the personality to lead headlines, leaning into a mid-90s retro feel.
summary: Narrow editorial serif with curvy italics, built for long-form copy and statement headlines with a retro feel.
description: A narrow serif by Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini designed for long-form editorial copy and statement headlines. Sixteen styles run across eight weights from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, plus a variable axis. Light cuts feel refined and fashion-friendly, the regular sits well in body text, and the heavy weights push lush, exaggerated curves for big titles.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Editorial New as precise and elegant, designed for long-form copy with enough personality to make a statement as a title. They frame it as giving designs a mid-90s retro feel that is rich and contemporary, with lush italics and luscious curves in the heavier weights. The family runs to sixteen styles plus variable cuts.

name: Editorial Old
designer: Mat Desjardins, Fred Wiltshire, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Italics, Serif and Variable. A spin-off of Editorial New that softens curves, droops ligatures and erodes terminals to evoke aged vintage adverts.
summary: An aged spin-off of Editorial New, with softer curves, droopier ligatures and eroded terminals for vintage editorial work.
description: A serif by Mat Desjardins, Fred Wiltshire and Francesca Bolognini that takes Editorial New and lets it grow old. Curves relax, ligatures droop, terminals soften and the result is a melted, eroded variant with a deep set of ligatures. Sixteen styles span eight weights with matching italics plus variable cuts. The accompanying campaign by Two Times Elliott leans into etched and moulded oddities, jewellery and trinkets.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Editorial Old as a fresh take on Editorial New, asking what the original would look like if it got old. They cite 1970s and 1980s vintage adverts as the original concept and credit Two Times Elliott with the visual campaign of refined curves, etched oddities and jewellery imagery.

name: Editorial Sans
designer: Andrea Biggio, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Humanist, Italics, Sans Serif, Text and Variable. A sans-serif companion to Editorial New that retains the same proportions and metrics, with true italics, which is uncommon in sans designs.
summary: Humanist sans companion to Editorial New, with retro-modern proportions and true italics across sixteen styles.
description: A humanist sans by Andrea Biggio, Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini that mirrors Editorial New's proportions and metrics in a sans-serif drawing. Sixteen styles across eight weights pair true italics with the uprights, giving a typeface that handles both editorial body and contemporary branding. Pangram pitch it as a balance of timeless charm and modern sophistication, equally happy in print and on screen.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Editorial Sans as bridging past and present in a retro-modern aesthetic, building on Editorial New but in sans-serif form. They highlight true italics as a rare feature in sans designs and frame the family as ideal for editorial layouts, branding, web and advertising.

name: Eiko
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Japanese, Serif and Variable. A high-contrast original serif with kana, hiragana and katakana, built to pair with kanji fonts in the Japanese mincho style.
summary: High-contrast display serif by Caio Kondo with Japanese kana, hiragana, katakana and basic kanji, inspired by Eiko Ishioka.
description: A high-contrast serif by Caio Kondo, named after the Japanese artist Eiko Ishioka and shaped with the same sense of purpose. Sixteen styles span eight weights from Thin to Heavy with full italics and a variable axis. Latin sits alongside Japanese kana, hiragana and katakana plus a 81-glyph educational kanji set, designed to pair with kanji fonts in the mincho style. Stylistic sets include circled and squared numbers, vertical writing, mathematical and meteorological symbols.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Eiko as an indisputable statement of elegance and refinement, citing Eiko Ishioka's multidisciplinary work as the typographic reference. They detail the full Japanese support, an 81-kanji educational set, full italics and an extensive set of OpenType features and stylistic alternates.

name: Formula
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif and Variable. A nine-axis grotesque family from Condensed to Extended, designed for impact and bold statements with race-inspired styling.
summary: 108-style grotesque width system by Mat Desjardins, running Condensed to Extended with full italics for bold campaigns.
description: A grotesque sans by Mat Desjardins built across six widths from Condensed to Extended, each with nine weights and matching italics, totalling 108 styles plus variable fonts. The drawing sits between racy display and workhorse text grotesque, so it can carry a poster headline as a Condensed Black Italic and still hold body copy at Normal Regular. Pangram pitch it as 'download, get set and go'.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Formula as complete and versatile, combining a bold racy look with the flexibility of a grotesque. They highlight the full width range from Condensed to Extended and the comprehensive weight ladder from Thin to Black, all with italics. Hugo Jourdan is credited on the V2 update.

name: Fragment
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif, Serif and Variable. A four-cut family bridging 19th-century lettering and contemporary typography: Sans, Serif, Glare (mid-serif revival) and Text.
summary: Four-cut serif and sans system by Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins, drawn from 19th-century lettering and signage.
description: PP Fragment is a four-style typographic system by Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins that pairs an elegant high-contrast Serif with a Glare mid-serif revival, a strong Sans and a Text cut. Each cut runs eighteen weights with italics and a variable axis, totalling 64 weights across the collection. Pangram describe it as bridging 19th-century letterforms and contemporary typography, drawn from vintage lettering and shop signs. 3D campaign visuals are by Arthur Brousseau-Regaudie.
foundry_blurb: Pangram present PP Fragment as their 35th release, born from vintage lettering and signs and gracefully oscillating between an elegant high-contrast Serif, a revival mid-serif called Glare, and a strong Sans. They cite 581 glyphs per weight and 32 distinct weights plus italics across the system.

name: Fraktion
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Grotesk, Italics, Monospace, Sans Serif and Variable. A grotesque drawn from vintage signage but redrawn as a versatile modern sans, paired with a fixed-width Mono companion.
summary: Contemporary grotesque by Juri Zaech with a matching monospace, drawn from vintage signage and built for screen and editorial.
description: A contemporary grotesque by Juri Zaech that reworks vintage signage lettering into a square, compact sans with ink traps and an exaggerated lowercase 'a' spur. PP Fraktion Sans pairs with PP Fraktion Mono, a fixed-width companion that loosens the drawing into something more playful than typical mono designs. Each cut runs seven weights with italics and variable fonts. OpenType features include case-sensitive punctuation, stylistic alternates, fractions and a slashed zero.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Fraktion Sans as a contemporary grotesque exploring the space between mechanical sturdiness and expressive clarity, with vintage signage as inspiration. They credit Juri Zaech and frame Fraktion Mono as the more informal companion in a genre that usually feels technical.

name: Frama
designer: Samuel Salminen, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Geometric, Italics, Sans Serif and Variable. A modernist geometric sans rooted in early 20th-century work like Futura and Avant Garde, offered in Display and Text cuts.
summary: Geometric sans by Samuel Salminen and team, rooted in Futura and Avant Garde, with Display and Text cuts across 28 styles.
description: A geometric sans by Samuel Salminen, Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini, drawn from circles, straight lines and considered counters in the lineage of Futura and Avant Garde. Frama balances precision with optical warmth and ships in two cuts, Display and Text, totalling 28 styles plus variable fonts. The system covers editorial headlines, branding, long-form reading and interface design, with stylistic alternates expanding its expressive range.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Frama as a contemporary geometric sans rooted in early 20th-century modernism, citing Futura's structural clarity and Avant Garde's expressive geometry. They frame it as balanced, calm and quietly confident, with Display and Text cuts and a dedicated microsite at pp-frama.com.

name: Fuji Sans
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Grotesk, Sans Serif and Variable. A bold grotesque with a slightly condensed feel and structured curves, drawn for branding and display work.
summary: Slightly condensed bold grotesque by Mat Desjardins, drawn for confident branding and display use.
description: A bold grotesque by Mat Desjardins with a slightly condensed feel and structured curves, sized down to four core weights from Light to Bold. Fuji Sans is shaped for branding and display rather than long-form text, leaning into a minimal structure with subtle personality. A variable font is included.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Fuji Sans as a beautiful bold sans-serif grotesk, with a slightly condensed feature and structured curves that suit bold designs and brands. They credit Mat Desjardins as designer.

name: Gatwick
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Display, Expressive, Humanist, Sans Serif and Variable. A wide incise typeface with tapered stems and sharp terminals, expanded in V2 with two high-contrast subfamilies.
summary: Wide incise display sans by Valerio Monopoli, with tapered stems, sharp terminals and three subfamilies for headlines.
description: A wide incise display sans by Valerio Monopoli that swaps the genre's usual neutrality for tapered stems, sharp terminals and exuberant detail. Gatwick V2 ships in three subfamilies, Jumbo, Jet and Glider, each with eight weights from Hairline to Bold and a variable cut, totalling 24 weights across the system. Jet and Glider extend the family with high-contrast cuts for elegant headlines and subtitles. Visuals are by Sergio Lairisa and Lakis Sobyra.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Gatwick as a wide incise typeface that blends a funky attitude with a refined spirit, working across vintage and contemporary aesthetics. They highlight V2's two new high-contrast families for elegant headlines and the airline-themed subfamily naming of Jumbo, Jet and Glider.

name: Gosha Sans
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Brutalist, Cyrillic, Geometric, Sans Serif and Variable. A geometric sans drawn from vintage Eastern European and Russian lettering, with strong lines and hard corners.
summary: Geometric brutalist sans by Mat Desjardins with eight weights, Cyrillic support and a stack of brutalist alternates.
description: A geometric sans by Mat Desjardins drawn from vintage Eastern European and Russian lettering, with hard corners and strong lines. Eight weights from Thin to Ultrabold cover branding and display work without sacrificing legibility at small sizes, and a deep set of alternates pushes the design into more brutalist territory. Cyrillic support is included.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Gosha Sans as inspired by vintage Eastern European and Russian currents, designed by Mat Desjardins. They highlight strong lines, hard corners and a set of alternates that lean brutalist, alongside Cyrillic support across eight weights.

name: Grafier
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Cyrillic, Expressive, Serif and Variable. A serif rooted in Baskerville but reshaped with uncompromisingly straight serifs that join inside the letters, plus flag terminals and squarish punctuation.
summary: Baskerville-derived serif by Alex Slobzheninov, with straight joining serifs, flag terminals and Text and Display contrasts.
description: A serif by Alex Slobzheninov that rethinks Baskerville for the digital era, with uncompromisingly straight serifs that join inside the letters, flag terminals and squarish punctuation. Ten styles split across five weights from Regular to Black in two contrast variations, Text and Display, with a two-axis variable font. Stylistic alternates can disconnect serifs to improve readability at smaller sizes, and Cyrillic is supported.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Grafier as a serif that rethinks classic design in the digital era, citing Baskerville as the original source. They highlight straight serifs that join inside the letters, flag terminals, squarish punctuation, two contrast variations and Cyrillic support.

name: Hatton
designer: Mat Desjardins, Morgane Vantorre, with Two Times Elliott
classification_notes: Pangram tag it Humanist, Italics, Serif and Variable. A humanist serif inspired by the street signage and shopfronts of Hatton Garden in London, with hand-rendered imperfections and pearlescent contrast.
summary: Humanist display serif drawn from Hatton Garden signage, with nine weights, true italics and a luxurious feel.
description: A humanist serif by Mat Desjardins and Morgane Vantorre, originally drawn in collaboration with London studio Two Times Elliott as a homage to Hatton Garden, the city's diamond quarter. The drawing distils street signage, shop fronts and hand-rendered imperfections into a variable family of nine upright weights and matching true italics, totalling eighteen styles plus a variable cut. Refined proportions and adjusted contrast give the dark weights an engaging rhythm, and a sackful of new glyphs widens the typographic range.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Hatton as a homage to Hatton Garden, designed in partnership with Two Times Elliott and updated with Morgane Vantorre. They cite the area's street signage, shop fronts and hand-rendered imperfections as references, and frame the latest version as a humanist serif befitted with newly crafted true italics and refined curves.

name: Neue Gstaad
designer: Ian Party, Francesca Bolognini, Mat Desjardins
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. Built as a complete width system that spans from Compressed through Expanded, with seven subfamilies and 112 styles plus variable cuts.
summary: A 112-style Swiss neo-grotesque width system by Ian Party, with seven subfamilies running from Compressed to Expanded.
description: A neo-grotesque width system by Ian Party, with assist from Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins. The family runs to 112 styles across seven widths from Compressed to Expanded, each with eight weights, matching italics and a variable cut. Pangram pitches it as a calm, efficient Swiss typeface for compact editorial settings through to architectural display, sitting between heritage Swiss reference points and a contemporary variable workflow. The Essential Collection bundles Regular, Italic, Bold and Bold Italic across all seven widths for teams who want one consistent system.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Neue Gstaad as a contemporary type family shaped by a distinctly Swiss sense of refinement, calm, confidence and precision. They credit Ian Party as designer and frame the family as moving beyond regional cliches into a broader expression of Swiss design rooted in clarity, discretion and quiet sophistication. They count 112 styles across 7 subfamilies, built as a complete future-facing variable ecosystem, loosely inspired by typographic giants but interpreted with restraint.

name: Paloma
designer: Mat Desjardins, Alex Slobzheninov, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Display, Expressive, Humanist, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. The family carries two subfamilies, Plain and Fizzy, that share a skeleton but split between humanist body settings and tighter geometric display cuts.
summary: A 28-style dual geo-humanist sans by Mat Desjardins, with a humanist Plain cut and a tighter, more geometric Fizzy cut.
description: A dual geo-humanist sans system by Mat Desjardins, with Alex Slobzheninov and Francesca Bolognini credited on the project. The 28-style family splits into two subfamilies: Plain, with open shapes, natural letterforms and balanced proportions for reading-comfort settings, and Fizzy, which tightens spacing, geometricises the shapes and crisps the details for large-size display. Each subfamily runs seven weights with italics, plus variable cuts. Pangram describe it as loosely inspired by Antique Olive, Johnston and Avenir, but treating those references lightheartedly. Useful where a brand needs both a comfortable text voice and a punchier display sibling without leaving the family.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe PP Paloma as a spritzy sans family of a dual geo-humanist nature. They credit it as designed for versatility, spanning humanist and geometric genres in two subfamilies, and loosely inspired by Antique Olive, Johnston and Avenir but treated with a lighthearted flair. They count 28 styles across two subfamilies of seven weights plus italics, all available as variable fonts, with OpenType features for professional typesetting and wide Latin language support.

name: PP Kyoto
classification_notes: Slab serif with bold slabs, soft teardrop terminals and prominent dots; began as a study of Japanese writing systems and ships with hiragana, katakana and Japanese punctuation alongside Latin.
summary: A bold slab serif with teardrop terminals and full hiragana and katakana support, built for headlines and bilingual editorial.
description: PP Kyoto is a slab serif with thick slabs, soft teardrop terminals and rhythmic dots that give headlines real bite. It runs across nine weights from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, eighteen styles plus a variable cut. The Latin set sits alongside complete hiragana, katakana and Japanese punctuation, with stylistic alternates including an alternate Q across 692 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe PP Kyoto as a slab serif that bridges tradition and modernity, designed to stand out in headlines through bold slabs, teardrop terminals and prominent dots. They trace its origin to a study of Japanese writing systems and frame it as a bilingual tool with full Latin, hiragana, katakana and Japanese punctuation across 692 glyphs. They highlight 9 weights with italics, stylistic alternates and an alternate Q for designers who want visual impact with technical consistency.

name: PP Lettra Mono
classification_notes: Monospaced serif with strong vertical contrast and diamond-shaped details; draws on serif typewriter fonts and botanical drawing classifications, with each glyph individually crafted for consistent weight on a fixed grid.
summary: A monospaced serif with strong vertical contrast and diamond details, drawing on typewriter fonts and botanical classifications.
description: PP Lettra Mono is a monospaced serif that explores modular spacing while keeping the bite of a high-contrast text serif. Eight weights run upright and italic across sixteen styles, with diamond-shaped details, alternate characters and an experimental set including unconventional ligatures, ordinals, double-width Oo and a sigil. Sits well in editorial systems and technical documents that want a typewriter mood without the period parody.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Lettra as a monospaced serif that explores the design challenges of modular spacing, with each character crafted to hold consistent weight and proportion on a fixed grid. They cite serif typewriter fonts and botanical drawing classifications as inspiration, and call out strong vertical contrast and diamond-shaped details. They flag eight weights with italics, wide language support, alternates and an experimental set with unusual ligatures, ordinal abbreviations, double-width Oo and a distinctive sigil.

name: PP Migra
classification_notes: High-contrast display serif with sharp, spiky terminals; weights span from a refined Extralight to a heavy Black, with gestural italics and a set of special ligatures.
summary: A spiky high-contrast display serif inspired by migratory birds, with eight weights and gestural italics for editorial and fashion work.
description: PP Migra is a sharp, high-contrast display serif inspired by the features of migratory birds. Eight weights run from an austere Extralight to a powerful Black, with sixteen total styles including italics and a variable font. The italic is more gestural than the upright and ships with special ligatures, which makes Migra a natural fit for fashion editorial, magazine covers and luxury identity work.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Migra as a spiky serif inspired by the features of migratory birds, spanning an austere and elegant light cut to a hawkish and powerful black. They flag a set of even more gestural italics and sundry special ligatures, and pitch the family as a way to add sparkle and grace to a design.

name: PP Model
subcategory: modular technical sans with mono and plastic cuts
classification_notes: Technical sans inspired by Tamiya plastic model kits, with four sub-families: Model Sans, Model Mono, Model Plastic and Model Plastic Line, each in 18 styles upright and italic with a variable cut. Total of 72 fonts.
summary: A 72-font technical system inspired by Tamiya model kits, with Sans, Mono, Plastic and Plastic Line cuts and Japanese support.
description: PP Model is a technical sans family inspired by the world of plastic model kits and the precision of Tamiya assembly instructions. Four sub-families run side by side: Model Sans, Model Mono, Model Plastic and Model Plastic Line, each in 18 styles with italics and a variable cut, for 72 fonts in total. The character set covers extended Latin, hiragana, katakana, Japanese punctuation and rarely-included measurement marks, which suits product, industrial and tech-flavoured identity work.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram say Model pays tribute to the world of plastic model kits and is rooted in the iconic Japanese brand Tamiya, celebrating the meticulous art of model-making through clean, modular forms. They describe four sub-families, from the default Model and monospaced variants to experimental Plastic styles, totalling 72 fonts in upright and italic. They flag extensive Latin support, Hiragana, Katakana, Japanese punctuation and measurement characters that are rarely found.

name: PP Monument
classification_notes: Grotesque sans inspired by brutalist architecture; 2025 update extends the family to five widths, each in nine weights, in upright and italic, totalling ninety styles plus a variable cut.
summary: A brutalist grotesque grown into a 90-style system across five widths and nine weights for branding, editorial and digital work.
description: PP Monument is a brutalist grotesque designed to make bold, structural statements. The 2025 update has grown it into a 90-style system: five widths from Condensed through Wide, each in nine weights, in upright and italic, with a variable cut. That range makes Monument flex from compressed posters to comfortably wide editorial settings without breaking voice across screens and scales.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Monument as a powerful grotesk inspired by the raw beauty and imposing forms of brutalist architecture, originally designed to make bold statements. They flag the major 2025 update as a step into one of their most expansive families, with five distinct widths, each in nine carefully tuned weights, across upright and italic styles. They pitch it as a workhorse with edge for branding systems, editorial layouts and digital experiences.

name: PP Mori
classification_notes: Gothic sans serif inspired by contemporary Japanese design; eleven weights upright and italic for twenty-two styles, each with 804 glyphs and OpenType features.
summary: A gothic sans inspired by contemporary Japanese design, with twenty-two styles, deep OpenType features and a quietly distinctive r and g.
description: PP Mori is a gothic sans serif influenced by contemporary Japanese graphic design, built to carry both editorial and branding workloads. Eleven weights run from Thin to Heavy with matching italics for twenty-two styles, and each font carries 804 glyphs with circled and squared numerals, sub and superscripts, Japanese-flavoured punctuation and a deep set of alternates. Look for the geometric r, the double-storey g and the slightly off G to spot it in the wild.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Mori as a versatile and sophisticated gothic sans serif inspired by contemporary Japanese design, best suited for editorial, graphic design and branding. They flag 22 styles from Thin to Black, each with 804 glyphs and OpenType features including circled and squared numbers, sub and superscript numbers, Japanese punctuation, mathematical symbols and many alternates. They invite designers to look at the geometric r, double-story g, G and numerals 6 and 9 as quiet points of difference.

name: PP Museum
classification_notes: Humanist sans-serif with subtle terminal flares that recede in heavier weights; references sketches of Optima on aged banknotes and vintage book and museum typography. Italics carry swashes and decorative terminals.
summary: A humanist sans with glyphic flares and swash italics, drawn from Optima sketches on banknotes and vintage book typography.
description: PP Museum is a humanist sans with glyphic terminal flares that thin out as weight builds, giving a stone-cut quality rare in contemporary sans. The reference points are Optima sketches found on aged banknotes and vintage book and museum-object typography, and the family runs nine weights with true italics carrying swashes and decorative terminals. It moves comfortably from refined body text to commanding headlines for cultural, editorial and luxury work.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Museum as a humanist sans-serif drawn from meticulous sketches of Optima found on aged banknotes, reinterpreted with a modern sensibility. They flag subtle terminal flares that echo a glyphic quality rarely seen in contemporary type, and reference research into vintage tomes, book covers, spines and museum-centric objects as the conceptual base. They highlight 9 weights with true italics that carry swashes and decorative terminals, an uncommon feature in sans-serif typefaces.

name: PP Neue Corp
classification_notes: Advertising and corporate system across eight widths and ten weights for 160 styles; Black and Condensed cuts reference Hamilton Mfg. Co. wood type, Wide Bolds reference Helvetica Extended, regular styles draw on Univers with softened details. Stylistic sets toggle between geometric neutrality and grotesque with spurs.
summary: A 160-style advertising and corporate system across eight widths, with wood type, Helvetica Extended and Univers references and toggle sets for spurs.
description: PP Neue Corp is a sprawling advertising and corporate system designed as an all-purpose creative tool. It runs across eight widths from Compressed to Extended and ten weights for 160 styles, with stylistic sets that toggle between a more geometric neutral cut and a grotesque cut with spurs. The references are explicit: Black and Condensed styles draw on Hamilton wood type, the Wide Bolds nod to Helvetica Extended, and the regular weights soften Univers for a friendlier tone.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Corp as a versatile typeface crafted as the ultimate creative tool for advertising, marketing and digital work, balancing eccentricity with neo-grotesque precision. They cite Hamilton Mfg. Co. wood type behind the Black and Condensed cuts, Helvetica Extended behind the Wide Bolds, and Univers with softened details behind the regular styles. They highlight stylistic alternates that swing the family between geometric neutrality and a bolder grotesque character with added spurs.

name: PP Neue Machina
classification_notes: Geometric display sans with monospace features and pronounced ink traps in heavier weights; ships in two cuts, Inktrap and Plain, each with italics and a variable font, for 36 styles total.
summary: A geometric display sans with monospace features and deep ink traps, in Inktrap and Plain cuts for technology and futurist work.
description: PP Neue Machina is a geometric display sans with monospace features and pronounced ink traps in its heavier weights, drawn from the aesthetics of robotics and machinery. It ships in two cuts, an aggressive Inktrap and a calmer Plain, each with italics and a variable font for 36 styles in total. Lighter weights blend in for body and interface use, while the heavy Inktrap cut carries the load for tech-flavoured headlines and product identity.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Machina as a powerful, meticulously crafted typeface with monospace and geometric features and apparent, deep ink traps in its heavier weights, inspired by the aesthetics of robotics and machines. They pitch it as versatile, blending in at lighter weights or carrying personality at heavier ones, suited for the future of technology. They flag an Inktrap cut and a Plain cut for more subtle work, each with italics and full variable support.

name: PP Neue Montreal
classification_notes: Versatile grotesque with the spirit of a display font; seven weights upright and italic for 14 styles, slightly tighter kerning, Cyrillic support. Inspired by Montreal's design city status, from Expo 67 to its contemporary scene.
summary: A grotesque with display spirit and tight kerning, in seven weights with italics and Cyrillic, pitched as a champion brand font.
description: PP Neue Montreal is a grotesque sans designed to act like a workhorse and a display face at once. Seven weights from Thin to Bold each have an italic, the kerning is slightly tighter than a default neutral grotesque, and the family carries Cyrillic support. The reference is Montreal as a design city, from Expo 67 to its current studio scene, and it has become one of the foundry's most-used identity faces.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Montreal as a versatile grotesque with the spirit of a display font, with 14 weights, slightly tighter kerning and Cyrillic support, pitched as your next champion font. They tie the design to what they see as Montreal's identity as a design city, from Expo 67 to its contemporary scene, and note its use by local brands including the rebrand of CF Montreal.

name: PP Neue Montreal Arabic
classification_notes: Arabic companion to PP Neue Montreal; seven weights plus a variable cut, with Cyrillic support carried over from the Latin family.
summary: An Arabic companion to Neue Montreal in seven weights with a variable cut, built for bilingual identity work.
description: PP Neue Montreal Arabic is the Arabic version of the foundry's most-used sans, drawn to sit beside the Latin family in bilingual identity work. It runs across seven weights plus a variable cut, carries Cyrillic support, and keeps the calm, slightly display-leaning tone of the parent grotesque. Use it where Arabic and Latin scripts need to feel like one voice rather than two systems.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram introduce this as the Arabic version of their most popular typeface, Neue Montreal, calling it a versatile grotesque with the spirit of a display font. They highlight 7 weights, Cyrillic support and the same Montreal-design-city heritage, and note its use by global and local brands.

name: PP Neue Montreal Mono
classification_notes: Monospaced version of PP Neue Montreal, with coding ligatures, multilingual support and a Button-mode feature; seven weights upright and italic for 14 styles plus a variable cut.
summary: A monospaced version of Neue Montreal with coding ligatures, multilingual support and a special button-mode for UI work.
description: PP Neue Montreal Mono is the monospace version of the foundry's bestseller, built for designers and developers who want their code voice to match their brand voice. It runs across seven weights upright and italic for 14 styles plus a variable cut, includes coding ligatures and a special Button-mode for UI work, and keeps the calm structural feel of the parent grotesque.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue Montreal Mono as the perfect fusion of style and functionality and the monospace version of their bestseller, Neue Montreal. They flag coding ligatures, a Button-mode, 14 styles including italics and multilingual support as the reasons to upgrade your typography for design and code projects.

name: PP Neue World
classification_notes: Display serif inspired by vintage display types manually squeezed to fit print sizes; six widths and eight weights for 48 cuts plus a variable font. Free to try.
summary: A super-serif with six widths and eight weights, inspired by vintage display types squeezed to fit the page.
description: PP Neue World is a free-to-try super-serif designed as a width system for headlines and posters. Six widths, from Super Condensed to Extended, each carry eight weights for 48 cuts plus a variable font. The thinner weights are soft and refined, the heavy ones lean into hard contrast and bold statements, and the whole family is drawn to mimic vintage display serifs that were squeezed to fit a given print measure.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue World as a free-to-try super-serif font family with 6 widths and 48 cuts, inspired by vintage display types that were manually squeezed to fit the print size. They highlight soft curves and refined details in the thinner weights and a hard contrast in the heavier weights, and pitch it as a beautiful modern serif that pays homage to its forerunners.

name: PP Neue York
classification_notes: Sans serif drawn from New York vernacular signage, from bodega awnings to subway tile mosaics and worn American gothics; three widths (Normal, Narrow, Condensed) drawn with deliberately different x-heights, eight weights with italics, 48 styles total.
summary: A 48-style sans drawn from New York vernacular signage, in three widths with deliberately different x-heights for headline and text.
description: PP Neue York is a vernacular gothic sans built from the layered visual noise of New York: bodega awnings, worn American gothics, subway tile mosaics and avenue and street rhythm. It runs across three widths drawn with deliberately different x-heights, with the Condensed taller and tighter for headlines and the Normal proportioned for text and continuous reading. Eight weights with italics in each width give a 48-style family that flexes from display to running copy without breaking voice.
foundry_blurb: Pangram Pangram describe Neue York as a sans serif shaped by the city's layered visual noise: bodega awnings, worn American gothics, mosaic station tiles and the rhythm of avenues and streets. They frame it as a contemporary type system with a vintage pulse, clean in structure and gently rough around the edges, with three widths whose density shifts the way the city shifts block by block. They flag the family as 48 styles, eight weights with italics across three widths, with x-heights tuned for display and text use.

name: PP Nikkei
designer: Caio Kondo, Francesca Bolognini, Mat Desjardins
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Geometric, Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif, Text, Variable. Built as five interlinked typographic systems: Upright, Italic, Journal, Maru, Pacific, Line, with a Crests glyph set. Marketed as a tribute to Japanese immigration to America.
summary: A multi-system display family by Inari Type and Pangram, built as a typographic tribute to Japanese immigration to America.
description: PP Nikkei is a sprawling display system by Caio Kondo, Francesca Bolognini and Mat Desjardins, structured as five interlinked subfamilies plus a Crests glyph set. The complete collection spans 72 styles with italics, variable cuts and a dedicated set of Japanese family crests. Pangram positions it as a tribute to Nikkei communities, drawing on hand-lettered ship signage, immigrant newspapers and family crests for the design references.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Nikkei as a tribute to Japanese immigration to America through typography, honouring Nikkei communities and their descendants. Five typographic systems were drawn, each carrying a distinct concept but interlaced by a shared historical narrative. References cited include hand-lettered ship signage, Nikkei newspapers and Japanese family crests.

name: PP Object Sans
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Geometric, Grotesk, Italics, Variable. Sixteen styles with italics and Cyrillic support. Pitched as a multifunctional workhorse blending Swiss neo-grotesk and geometric traits.
summary: A geometric neo-grotesque workhorse by Alex Slobzheninov, sized for branding, packaging, posters and small body text.
description: Object Sans is a 16-style sans by Alex Slobzheninov that blends Swiss neo-grotesk discipline with geometric construction. Seven weights run from Thin to Black, each paired with a Slanted cut, and the regular weight is tuned for small body text. Cyrillic support and a variable axis make it a useful default for identity systems, packaging and on-screen interfaces.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Object Sans as a contemporary type family that combines the best qualities of Swiss neo-grotesks and geometric fonts. They pitch it as a multifunctional workhorse for printed and on-screen contexts, including logo design, brand identities, websites, packaging, posters and headlines, with the regular weight tuned for small body text.

name: PP Pangaia
designer: Samuel Salminen, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Italics, Organic, Serif, Variable. Eighteen styles across nine weights with italics and a variable cut. Marketed as a free to try organic serif inspired by natural forms.
summary: An organic serif by Samuel Salminen with Pangram, drawn from natural forms and shipped in 18 styles plus a variable cut.
description: Pangaia is an organic serif of nine weights and matching italics, designed by Samuel Salminen with Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini. The forms blend traditional serif structure with curves and swashes drawn from leaves, riverbanks and rolling landscape. With 18 styles, swash italics and a variable cut, it stretches across book setting, branding and editorial display.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Pangaia as a free to try typeface that blends the splendour of traditional serifs with the rough, raw beauty of nature. They cite leaves, riverbanks and mountain slopes as inspirations, and frame the family as a celebration of our connection to the Earth across 18 styles, including striking swashes and powerful italics.

name: PP Pangram Sans
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Geometric, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. Six widths, 24 styles each, totalling 144 styles. Variable slanting from Reclined to Italic, with Cyrillic support.
summary: A 144-style geometric sans by Pangram with six widths and a variable slant axis from Reclined to Italic.
description: Pangram Sans is a geometric workhorse by Mat Desjardins and Valerio Monopoli, built across six widths and 24 styles each for 144 styles in total. A fully variable slant axis runs from Reclined to Italic, and Cyrillic support is included. The width range, from Compressed up to Normal, covers everything from masthead headlines to text setting in one system.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Pangram Sans as a powerful and extensive geometric workhorse, designed to be both bold and intense and subtle and flexible. They highlight the fully variable slant from Reclined to Italic, Cyrillic support, and the inspiration drawn from some of the greatest geometric typefaces.

name: PP Pangram Sans Rounded
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Geometric, Italics, Sans Serif. The rounded sibling of Pangram Sans, with the same 144-style six-width architecture and Cyrillic support.
summary: The rounded sibling of Pangram Sans, again with 144 styles across six widths, tuned for friendlier brand work.
description: Pangram Sans Rounded is the softened version of Pangram Sans by Mat Desjardins and Valerio Monopoli. It keeps the same six-width, 144-style architecture and Cyrillic support, with rounded terminals giving the heavier weights a more approachable feel. Useful where Pangram Sans would feel too austere, particularly in consumer brand work, packaging and apps.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Pangram Sans Rounded as the more fun and playful sibling of Pangram Sans, an extensive geometric family of 144 styles with a softer side. They note that the bolder weights are where the typeface becomes most distinctive, and the family includes Cyrillic support and free to try licensing.

name: PP Pier Sans
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif. Ten styles across five weights with italics. Pitched as a slightly off geometric workhorse for everyday text and design needs.
summary: A ten-style geometric grotesque by Mat Desjardins, drawn to read well at both display and small text sizes.
description: Pier Sans is an early Pangram release by Mat Desjardins, structured as five weights with matching italics. The forms sit between geometric and grotesque, with detail tuned to hold up at both large and small sizes. It works as a quiet brand and interface sans where you want a touch of personality without leaving the workhorse register.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Pier as a modern, structured Grotesk with details that give it a unique personality. They built it to look as good at large display sizes as at very small ones, designed to fit everyday text and design needs.

name: PP Rader
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Brutalist, Expressive, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. Sixteen styles across eight weights with italics and a variable cut. Drawn from vintage road signage and turn-of-the-century industrial grotesques.
summary: An expressive industrial grotesque by Valerio Monopoli, drawn from vintage road signage in 16 styles plus a variable cut.
description: Rader is a 16-style industrial grotesque by Valerio Monopoli, with weights from Hairline to Bold and matching italics. The drawing pulls from vintage German road signage and a debut-de-siecle aesthetic, sitting between editorial display and an expressive workhorse. Round inner counters give it a printed feel without losing its modern read.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Rader as both an expressive display typeface and a readable editorial tool, with 16 weights from a stripped-down hairline to a decorative bold. They cite vintage signage systems as the brief, framing the typeface as an attempt to bring craft back to wayfinding while spicing up the genre of industrial grotesques.

name: PP Radio Grotesk
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif. Sixteen styles across eight weights with italics and Cyrillic support. Pitched as a contemporary take on a classic Grotesk.
summary: A contemporary grotesque by Jack Harvatt and Mat Desjardins, 16 styles across eight weights with Cyrillic support.
description: Radio Grotesk is a 16-style contemporary grotesque by Jack Harvatt and Mat Desjardins. Eight weights run from Ultralight to Black with matching italics, and the family includes Cyrillic support. Established uppercase forms sit alongside more sophisticated lowercase, making it a steady choice for editorial and brand systems that want a current grotesque without trend baggage.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Radio Grotesk as a typeface that brings contemporary characteristics to a classic Grotesk style, pairing established majuscules with sophisticated minuscules. They highlight the eight weights as easily adaptable to any project.

name: PP Right Didone
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Display, Italics, Serif, Variable. Eight subfamilies with seven cuts each, 56 styles total. Built by adding serifs to Right Sans rather than as a historical revival, giving it a tall x-height and grotesk-like style range.
summary: A contemporary didone by Alex Slobzheninov, 56 styles built by adding serifs to Right Sans rather than reviving a historical model.
description: Right Didone is a 56-style high-contrast serif by Alex Slobzheninov, part of the Right superfamily. It is structured as eight cuts, including Tall, Tight, Compact, Casual, Wide, Narrow, Spatial and Italic, each with seven weights. Pangram frame the design as Right Sans with serifs added rather than a historical revival, giving it the tall x-height and broad style coverage of a grotesque rather than a classic didone.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Didone as the classic serif reinvented through a contemporary lens, beaming elegance with a slightly quirky manner. They make the point that it is more than not a historical revival, having been created by adding serifs to Right Sans, which is why it carries a tall x-height and a grotesk-like range of styles.

name: PP Right Gothic
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Gothic, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. Seven subfamilies of 14 styles each, 98 styles total: 7 weights x 7 widths x 20 degree italic. Cyrillic support included. Drawn with pronounced contrast that nods to early pre-Swiss sans typefaces.
summary: A 98-style gothic sans by Alex Slobzheninov with seven weights, seven widths and pronounced thick-thin contrast.
description: Right Gothic is a 98-style sans by Alex Slobzheninov, built as seven weights across seven widths with italic cuts. Compared to the wider Right Grotesk, it carries a pronounced thick-thin contrast that nods to early pre-Swiss sans typefaces. Smooth ink traps and a contemporary feel keep it usable as a body voice as well as a headline. Cyrillic support is included.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Gothic as a large multi-purpose variable family with a unique voice rooted in distinct details, smooth ink traps and a contemporary feel. They explain that, unlike most modern sans serifs, Right Gothic features a clearly pronounced contrast between thick and thin strokes, referencing the pre-Swiss gothic genre of sans typefaces. The family covers 98 styles with optional custom contrast axes on request.

name: PP Right Grotesk
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. Eleven subfamilies of 14 styles each, 130 styles total: 7 display cuts plus 5 text cuts. Cyrillic support included. The flagship neo-grotesque of the Right superfamily.
summary: A 130-style neo-grotesque by Alex Slobzheninov, with display and text cuts across seven widths and seven weights.
description: Right Grotesk is the anchor of Pangram's Right superfamily, a 130-style neo-grotesque by Alex Slobzheninov. Seven display cuts cover the wider Right system shapes, and five additional text cuts are tuned for paragraph reading. Moderate contrast, smooth curves and slightly unusual anatomy give it more personality than a strict workhorse, with Cyrillic support throughout.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Grotesk as a typeface that blends the neutrality and functionality of workhorses with a good touch of distinctive personality. They cite fine details, smooth curves, moderate contrast and slightly unusual anatomy, and frame it as not trendy and not timeless either, designed to be just Right.

name: PP Right Grotesk Mono
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Grotesk, Italics, Monospace, Sans Serif, Variable. Fourteen styles across seven weights with italics. Pitched as a softer, more playful monospace counterpart to Right Grotesk.
summary: A 14-style monospace by Alex Slobzheninov, pairing Right Grotesk's voice with playful technical details.
description: Right Grotesk Mono is a 14-style monospace by Alex Slobzheninov, built as seven weights from Fine to Black with matching italics. Its compact proportions read well in paragraphs while staying recognisable at large sizes, giving it a friendly technological feel. It pairs cleanly with Right Grotesk and Right Serif Mono, and slots into the broader Right superfamily.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Grotesk Mono as a typeface that spices up the rigid structure of the monospaced genre with playful details and subtle twists. They highlight its fairly compact proportions for paragraph reading and its place inside the wider Right superfamily, with Right Grotesk and Right Serif Mono cited as natural pairings.

name: PP Right Sans
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Cyrillic, Italics, Sans Serif, Variable. Seven subfamilies of 14 styles each, 98 styles total. A high-contrast sans sibling to Right Grotesk and Right Gothic; together the three families exceed 300 fonts. Cyrillic support included.
summary: A 98-style high-contrast sans by Alex Slobzheninov, sitting between Right Grotesk and Right Gothic in the Right superfamily.
description: Right Sans is a 98-style high-contrast sans by Alex Slobzheninov, structured as seven widths and seven weights with italic cuts. Pangram describe it as fancier than your average sans without crossing into serif territory. Together with Right Grotesk and Right Gothic it forms a pairing system of more than 300 styles, with Cyrillic support across the family.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Sans as just a tiny bit fancier than your favourite serif fonts, except it keeps it sans, available across weights, widths and italic angles in classic and variable formats. They position it as the high-contrast sibling of Right Grotesk and Right Gothic, with the three families together giving over 300 fonts perfect for pairing.

name: PP Right Serif
classification_notes: Pangram tags it Display, Italics, Serif, Variable. Eight subfamilies of seven styles each, 56 styles total. Drawn by adding serifs to Right Gothic, inheriting moderate contrast, a tall x-height and grotesk-style style coverage rather than a classical revival lineage.
summary: A 56-style contemporary serif by Alex Slobzheninov, drawn by adding serifs to Right Gothic rather than reviving a historical model.
description: Right Serif is a 56-style serif by Alex Slobzheninov, built as seven weights across seven widths plus a dedicated Italic family. It originated as a sans, growing serifs onto Right Gothic, which is why it carries a moderate contrast, tall x-height and the broad style coverage more typical of a grotesque. Most useful at large and medium sizes, with regular cuts that hold up in smaller paragraphs.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Serif as a funky multitool, a spiced-up workhorse stretching between high function and unique personality. They make the point that, unlike many serifs rooted in history, Right Serif originated as a sans-serif growing serifs from Right Gothic, inheriting a tall x-height and a wide range of variable styles across seven weights and seven widths.

name: PP Right Serif Mono
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Monospace, Serif and Variable. It sits inside the wider Right superfamily, taking the proportions of Right Serif and refitting every glyph to a single fixed width.
summary: A serif monospace by Alex Slobzheninov that takes the Right superfamily into fixed-width territory without the usual deadpan mono feel.
description: A serif monospace built off the wider Right system, with seven weights from Fine to Black plus matching italics for fourteen styles in a variable file. Equal widths give it the grid discipline of code, but the proportions lean into the quirks of the Right family so paragraphs and large display lines keep some warmth. Useful for identity systems that want a bit of tech meets editorial, creative coding work, zines and infographics, and it pairs cleanly with Right Serif or Right Grotesk Mono.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Right Serif Mono as a monospace with the right twist, bringing the liveliness of the Right superfamily into the monospaced genre. They say it embraces the twisted proportions of every character fitted to one width while staying easy to read in paragraphs and distinctive at display size, and works equally well as a primary face or paired with Right Serif and Right Grotesk Mono.

name: PP Right Slab
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Display, Italics, Serif and Variable. It is the slab serif sibling of Right Grotesk, sharing low contrast and a large x-height, with seven weights across seven widths.
summary: A wide-ranging slab serif by Alex Slobzheninov, sibling to Right Grotesk, with 49 styles across seven weights and seven widths.
description: A low-contrast slab serif built as the serif brother of Right Grotesk, with chunky bracketed serifs and a large x-height. Seven weights run across seven widths from Tall to Spatial for 49 cuts in total, plus an italic family, all available in a variable file. Most at home at display size but the regular weights handle paragraphs, with a campaign by Studio Nari leaning into a cinematic, slightly Tarantino-flavoured mood.
foundry_blurb: Pangram call Right Slab the big boi of the family, massive at first glance and quickly familiar. They say its heavy styles unapologetically occupy the space with chunky serifs while light and narrow cuts leave more air, that it is the serif brother of Right Grotesk inheriting low contrast, large x-height and a ton of variable styles across seven weights and seven widths, and they paired the release with an American Psycho flavoured campaign from Studio Nari.

name: PP Stellar Sans
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Brutalist, Grotesk and Sans Serif. A slightly condensed sans with a tall x-height, designed for tight layouts and high-density settings.
summary: A slightly condensed grotesque sans by Mat Desjardins with a tall x-height, built for tight layouts and dense typographic systems.
description: A condensed sans serif drawn for legibility in tight measures, with a tall x-height that holds clarity at small sizes. Four weights from Light to Bold cover most hierarchy needs, and the narrow proportions make it useful for editorial spreads, mobile interfaces, packaging and poster blocks where every column counts. The brutalist edge keeps it from feeling generic when scaled up for display.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Stellar as a slightly condensed sans serif with a tall x-height for legibility and personality, designed for clarity, impact and efficient use of space. They frame it as inspired by the tension between the galactic and the atomic, with four weights built to give purpose to ideas at any scale.

name: PP Supply
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Geometric, Monospace, Sans Serif and Variable. A two-cut system with a Sans family and a matching Monospace family, sharing the same skeleton.
summary: A geometric sans plus matching monospace by Mat Desjardins, drawn from industrial design and architecture for utilitarian, structured layouts.
description: A two-part system pairing a geometric sans with a monospace cut on the same skeleton, eight weights each from Thin to Black with a variable file per cut. Precise curves and sharp angles give it an industrial feel that suits technical brands, packaging, dashboards and code-adjacent work where you want the structure of a mono to live alongside text in the sans. A reliable workhorse rather than a flashy display face.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Supply as a solid and versatile typeface inspired by industrial design and architecture, with precise curves and sharp angles. They pitch it as one for every designer's toolbox, available in two cuts, a Sans Serif and a classic Monospace, eight styles each plus variable fonts.

name: PP Talisman
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Display, Humanist, Italics, Sans Serif and Variable. A five-width, nine-weight humanist sans with matching obliques, organised across Compressed, Condensed, Narrow, Compact and Normal widths.
summary: A 90-style humanist display sans by Valerio Monopoli, spanning five widths and nine weights with matching obliques.
description: A humanist display sans built for breadth, with five widths from Compressed to Normal and nine weights from Thin to Black, plus matching obliques, totalling 90 styles each carrying 687 glyphs. The casual, stripped-back skeleton keeps it minimal at body size, while the wide range across widths lets brands run from compact captions to full-bleed posters within one system. Visuals for the release are by Sergio Lairisa.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Talisman as a lucky font to carry around, framing it as an attempt to harness luck through hyper-minimalist design with an effortless, almost simplistic structure. They say its generous range of weights and widths ensures variety of expression, made more vibrant by a dashing oblique counterpart, and pitch it as an amulet a designer keeps in their pocket as both first and last creative resort.

name: PP Telegraf
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Brutalist, Grotesk, Italics and Variable. A modern grotesk whose counters become more rectangular as weight increases, with eight weights and two italic systems at 15 degrees and 30 degrees.
summary: A brutalist grotesk by Nick Losacco and Samuel Salminen with rectangular counters in the heavy weights and two italic angles for slant or shock.
description: A modern grotesk that swaps grotesque softness for rigid angles, with counters becoming more rectangular as weight increases to keep small sizes legible and big sizes punchy. Twenty-four styles split across an Upright, a comfortable 15 degree Slanted for editorial, and a more aggressive 30 degree Oblique for graphic disruption, all with a variable file. Suits tech-forward brands, editorial systems and poster work that wants structure rather than warmth.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Telegraf as grotesk with grit, combining the forms of mid-century grotesks with rigid angles. They say its counters become more rectangular as weight increases to help on-screen viewing at small sizes and impact at large ones, framing it as a well-planted modern grotesk with a touch of brutalism, with a comfortable 15 degree Slanted for editorial work and an extreme 30 degree Oblique for a more brutalist, graphic approach.

name: PP Valve
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Grotesk, Italics, Sans Serif and Variable. A two-family system pairing an ink-trapped Plain workhorse with a Stencil companion that exposes the modular structure, with three stylistic sets covering monospaced, humanist and cursive flavours.
summary: An industrial grotesque by Valerio Monopoli with an ink-trapped Plain family and a matching Stencil cut, plus monospaced, humanist and cursive stylistic sets.
description: A two-family sans serif system with a Plain cut built for small-size readability through ink traps and a Stencil cut that exposes the modular bones underneath. Sixteen styles per family with italics, plus three main stylistic sets covering a blocky semi-monospaced variant, a humanist style and a cursive option, each with contextual ligatures that adapt to tracking. Useful range from bold branding and signage to refined editorial.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Valve as a versatile collection that blends grotesque, geometric, gestural, industrial, monospaced and cursive sans serif styles into a unified ensemble. They highlight an ink-trapped workhorse for small-size readability and a striking stencil companion that reveals the modular structure, plus three stylistic sets with contextual ligatures that adapt to tracking, with visuals by Draaav, 3D by Reggy and motion by Andres Gutierrez Pineros.

name: PP Watch
designer: Niklas Herrmann, Mat Desjardins, Francesca Bolognini
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Brutalist, Grotesk, Sans Serif and Variable. A wide-set sans inspired by Swiss watchmaking and engraved dial typography, with integrated small caps and a micro-serif stylistic set.
summary: A wide luxury sans by Niklas Herrmann, Mat Desjardins and Francesca Bolognini, drawn from Swiss watch dial typography with built-in small caps.
description: A wide-set sans with calm horizontal posture and tight modulation, drawn from engraved watch face typography and skeleton dials in Swiss museum archives. Nine weights from Thin to Black with 994 glyphs each in a single variable file, including integrated small caps for traditional dial behaviour and an optional micro-serif stylistic set that nods to vintage ink traps. Aimed at luxury brands, high-end editorial and identity work that wants understated craft.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Watch as understated and overdelivered, a typeface born from the intricate world of horology and inspired by the fine detailing of Swiss timepieces and engraved watch face typography. They say it balances mechanical structure with luxurious refinement through a wide-set posture and tight modulation, with built-in small caps as a nod to traditional dials and a micro-serif stylistic set that echoes vintage ink-trap techniques, developed with Short Notice Studio.

name: PP Woodland
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Organic and Serif. A curvy serif with around 2,500 glyphs per style, soft modulation and quirky details such as a twisted lowercase e.
summary: A curvy organic serif by Mat Desjardins in six weights, with a quirky lowercase e, ligatures and curvier alternates for editorial and brand work.
description: A serif with smooth, organic curves and quiet quirks, sitting between elegance and warmth across six weights from Ultralight to Heavy. Each style carries roughly 2,500 glyphs, with ligatures, old-style figures and a stylistic set of curvier alternates so the same family can hold a calm body text or a more theatrical headline. Useful for luxury brands, literary magazines and contemporary editorial.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Woodland as a beautiful, curvy, versatile serif typeface with six weights and around 2,500 glyphs per style. They highlight the soft quirkiness in details such as the lowercase e, saying it gives the type a great personality in titles and body texts while keeping legibility, and note the elegant ligatures and curvier alternates available when more personality is needed.

name: PP Writer
subcategory: Renaissance serif with Text and Display cuts
classification_notes: Pangram lists it under Cyrillic, Display, Italics, Serif, Text and Variable. A French Renaissance inspired serif with three cuts, Text, Display and Italic, and Cyrillic support that follows Cyrillic rather than Latin tradition on letters such as Tse and Sha.
summary: A Renaissance-inspired serif by Fred Wiltshire and Mat Desjardins with Text, Display and Italic cuts, nine weights, Cyrillic support and 30 styles in total.
description: A French Renaissance serif with proportions, contrast and stress drawn from sixteenth-century type, organised into a Text cut for 6 to 14 point body, a Display cut that sharpens contrast and serifs for big sizes, and a lively Italic. Thirty styles in total with around 744 glyphs each, plus a Cyrillic that respects local tradition on letters like Tse and Sha rather than copying Latin shapes. A workhorse for print and editorial that still has a bit of ink-spill drama in the heavier weights.
foundry_blurb: Pangram describe Writer as a French Renaissance inspired serif, an elegant and dynamic face including three cuts, text and display styles plus a playful italic, with nine weights per cut and 744 glyphs each. They frame it as a legible, traditional text face that pushes Renaissance type into the 21st century, with a Cyrillic that follows Cyrillic tradition rather than Latin motifs on letters such as Tse and Sha.
